[2023-05-28 18:12] George Washington owned over 300 slaves at the time of his death
nodes.description: George Washington inherited his first slaves from his father at the age of 11 and continued to acquire more throughout his life, owning over 300 slaves at his death.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] George Washington began to question the morality of slavery during his lifetime
nodes.description: Throughout his life, George Washington started to question the morality of slavery and freed some of his slaves in his will.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] George Washington did not free all of his slaves due to economic concerns
nodes.description: George Washington did not free all of his slaves when he died because he was concerned about the economic loss that would result from freeing them, as they were a significant source of labor on his estate.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] George Washington's will provided for the emancipation of certain slaves
nodes.description: In his will, George Washington freed some of his slaves and provided for the emancipation of any enslaved person who had served him for at least 30 years or who was above the age of 60, with conditions for service until ages 26 for males and 24 for females.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] George Washington left 123 slaves in the custody of his wife Martha
nodes.description: George Washington left 123 slaves in the custody of his wife Martha and instructed her to provide for them, but not to free them.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] Martha Washington may have kept some slaves to protect them from re-enslavement
nodes.description: It is possible that Martha Washington kept some slaves inherited from her husband to protect them from re-enslavement, as laws at the time required freed slaves to leave the state within a certain period or risk being re-enslaved.

nodes.title: [2023-05-28 18:12] Martha Washington passed away in 1802
nodes.description: Martha Washington passed away in 1802, during a time when many states had laws affecting freed slaves.
